title: Global Kochen-Specker Contextuality Without Local Contextuality and Generalized Bell Nonlocality
abstract: A set of quantum data can look classical in every local test and still fail to admit a single classical explanation of the whole composite system. We formulate this failure as global contextuality. Here global means global in the physical sense of the whole multipartite system, not the local/global terminology of sheaf theory. Each party's local statistics are noncontextual and each measured multipartite context admits a generalized local hidden-variable description, but the GLHV block descriptions cannot be promoted to a single noncontextual hidden-variable model for the whole system. Three bipartite constructions exhibit this separation. A polarization-path construction gives a direct global obstruction. A qubit-qutrit KCBS construction gives an algebraic scenario-level example, with explicit formulas for the unconditional KCBS operator, the correlation-polytope constraints, and the postselected violation. A flagged qutrit Werner-local state gives a state-level example: the state is entangled and local for all projective measurements, its local qutrit marginals do not violate KCBS, yet postselection rules out a single GNCHV model. We also spell out the classical composition lemma: classical conditional hidden variables can be absorbed into a larger hidden variable, whereas quantum contextual data need not allow such a factorization. Within the general Bell-type framework considered here, with arbitrary parties and arbitrary local compatible contexts, but no cross-party joint measurements, the absence of local contextuality and GLHV-type generalized Bell nonlocality does not imply the existence of a global noncontextual hidden-variable model. Global contextuality is thus a compositional obstruction to classical explanation.
summary: This paper introduces the concept of global contextuality, demonstrating that multipartite systems can exhibit nonclassical behavior that cannot be explained by a single global hidden-variable model, even when all local parts appear classical. Through three different bipartite constructions, the author shows that nonclassicality can emerge as a compositional obstruction when local classical descriptions cannot be 'glued' together.
main_problem: Investigating the existence of 'global contextuality,' where a multipartite system fails to admit a single global noncontextual hidden-variable model even though all local subsystems are noncontextual and all multipartite contexts admit a generalized local hidden-variable description.
main_result: The paper proves that the absence of local contextuality and generalized Bell nonlocality does not imply the existence of a global noncontextual hidden-variable model, establishing global contextuality as a compositional obstruction to classicality.
method: The author uses an operational approach involving three bipartite constructions (polarization-path, qubit-qutrit, and flagged qutrit Werner-local) and employs linear programming, quantifier elimination, and postselection to identify violations of global hidden-variable models.
model_or_system: The study considers various bipartite measurement scenarios, including a 2x4 polarization-path system, a 2x3 qubit-qutrit system with KCBS observables, and a qutrit Werner-local state with a product flag.
key_observables: KCBS operator, polarization observables, and a Hardy-type linear witness.
important_parameters: Schmidt coefficients (h0, h1), weighting parameter (a), and the KCBS noncontextuality threshold (c0 <= 0.850651).
main_assumptions: The claim regarding the absence of generalized nonlocality is restricted to specific measurement scenarios without cross-party joint measurements.
figures_summary: The paper includes a summary table (Table I) classifying the three different separation constructions based on their hidden resources and qualifications.
paper_structure: The paper defines the hierarchy of classicality, establishes a gluing criterion for global contextuality, and then presents three distinct constructions (incompatibility-based, algebraic/postselected, and state-level) to demonstrate the separation of scales.
why_interesting: It provides a new fundamental classification of nonclassicality (global contextuality) that is distinct from standard Bell nonlocality and local Kochen-Specker contextuality, which is relevant for understanding the limits of classical simulations of multipartite quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
